The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor value layout. S1 is after local ascending sort on each processor. Each subsequent state records one synchronized bitonic compare-split round, followed by a final local reverse on each processor.

- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 31.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Dense N x N matrix, block-row distributed:
#   each rank holds m = N / P consecutive rows
#   rank r owns global rows [r*m .. r*m + m-1]
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure bar(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    N = length(local[0])

    k = N - 1
    while k >= 0:
        (piv_row, piv_val) = global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k)

        if piv_row != k:
            swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, piv_row, k, N)

        barrier()

        piv_owner = row_owner(k, m)
        pivot_row = broadcast_row(local, rank, P, piv_owner, k, N)

        if abs(pivot_row[k]) <= 1e-8:
            barrier()
            k = k - 1
            continue

        r = 0
        while r < m:
            gi = rank * m + r                  # global row index
            if gi < k:                         # only rows above the pivot
                factor = trunc_toward_zero(local[r][k] / pivot_row[k])
                c = 0
                while c <= k:                  # touch only the leading (k+1) columns
                    local[r][c] = local[r][c] - factor * pivot_row[c]
                    c = c + 1
            r = r + 1

        barrier()
        k = k - 1

    return local


procedure global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k):
    m = length(local)
    # Local scan over eligible rows owned here (gi <= k)
    best_abs = -1
    best_idx = -1
    best_val = 0

    r = 0
    while r < m:
        gi = rank * m + r
        if gi <= k:
            v = local[r][k]
            a = abs(v)
            if a > best_abs or (a == best_abs and gi > best_idx):
                best_abs = a
                best_idx = gi
                best_val = v
        r = r + 1

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, (best_abs, best_idx, best_val))

        (p_abs, p_idx, p_val) = peer
        if p_abs > best_abs or (p_abs == best_abs and p_idx > best_idx):
            best_abs = p_abs
            best_idx = p_idx
            best_val = p_val

        j = j * 2

    return (best_idx, best_val)


procedure swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, p, q, N):
    if p == q: return

    m = length(local)
    op = row_owner(p, m)
    oq = row_owner(q, m)
    lp = p - op * m
    lq = q - oq * m

    if op == oq:
        if rank == op:
            tmp = local[lp]; local[lp] = local[lq]; local[lq] = tmp
        return

    if rank == op:
        peer_row = sendrecv(oq, local[lp])
        local[lp] = peer_row
    elif rank == oq:
        peer_row = sendrecv(op, local[lq])
        local[lq] = peer_row
    else:
        # Non-owners do nothing, but keep call sites symmetric
        noop = None


procedure broadcast_row(local, rank, P, owner, k, N):
    m = length(local)
    if rank == owner:
        lr = k - owner * m
        buf = copy_row(local[lr])         # send an owned copy
    else:
        buf = EMPTY_ROW()

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, buf)
        # If I don't have the row yet, take the peer's copy
        if is_empty(buf) and not is_empty(peer):
            buf = peer
        j = j * 2

    return buf


procedure row_owner(global_row, m):
    return floor(global_row / m)

procedure trunc_toward_zero(x):
    if x >= 0: return floor(x)
    else: return -floor(-x)

procedure copy_row(row):
    out = new list(length(row))
    i = 0
    while i < length(row):
        out[i] = row[i]; i = i + 1
    return out

procedure EMPTY_ROW():
    return new list(0)

procedure is_empty(x):
    return length(x) == 0
```

Input size N = 64, processors P = 8.
Starting values: a 64 x 64 upper-triangular integer matrix, using 0-based row and column indices, with entry A[i][j] = 0 when j < i, A[i][j] = 1 when j = i, and A[i][j] = ((17*i + 31*j + 64 + 8) mod 3) - 1 when j > i. It is distributed by contiguous row blocks of 8 rows per processor and stored row-major within each processor.

Definitions:
- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor flattened row-major matrix layout, where each processor stores (N/P) full rows of an N x N matrix. Each subsequent state records one full pivot step k (descending from N-1 to 0): global pivot selection, optional distributed row swap, pivot-row broadcast, and elimination of rows above the pivot (or skip when pivot is zero).
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 7.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Dense N x N matrix, block-row distributed:
#   each rank holds m = N / P consecutive rows
#   rank r owns global rows [r*m .. r*m + m-1]
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure bar(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    N = length(local[0])

    k = N - 1
    while k >= 0:
        (piv_row, piv_val) = global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k)

        if piv_row != k:
            swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, piv_row, k, N)

        barrier()

        piv_owner = row_owner(k, m)
        pivot_row = broadcast_row(local, rank, P, piv_owner, k, N)

        if abs(pivot_row[k]) <= 1e-8:
            barrier()
            k = k - 1
            continue

        r = 0
        while r < m:
            gi = rank * m + r                  # global row index
            if gi < k:                         # only rows above the pivot
                factor = trunc_toward_zero(local[r][k] / pivot_row[k])
                c = 0
                while c <= k:                  # touch only the leading (k+1) columns
                    local[r][c] = local[r][c] - factor * pivot_row[c]
                    c = c + 1
            r = r + 1

        barrier()
        k = k - 1

    return local


procedure global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k):
    m = length(local)
    # Local scan over eligible rows owned here (gi <= k)
    best_abs = -1
    best_idx = -1
    best_val = 0

    r = 0
    while r < m:
        gi = rank * m + r
        if gi <= k:
            v = local[r][k]
            a = abs(v)
            if a > best_abs or (a == best_abs and gi > best_idx):
                best_abs = a
                best_idx = gi
                best_val = v
        r = r + 1

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, (best_abs, best_idx, best_val))

        (p_abs, p_idx, p_val) = peer
        if p_abs > best_abs or (p_abs == best_abs and p_idx > best_idx):
            best_abs = p_abs
            best_idx = p_idx
            best_val = p_val

        j = j * 2

    return (best_idx, best_val)


procedure swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, p, q, N):
    if p == q: return

    m = length(local)
    op = row_owner(p, m)
    oq = row_owner(q, m)
    lp = p - op * m
    lq = q - oq * m

    if op == oq:
        if rank == op:
            tmp = local[lp]; local[lp] = local[lq]; local[lq] = tmp
        return

    if rank == op:
        peer_row = sendrecv(oq, local[lp])
        local[lp] = peer_row
    elif rank == oq:
        peer_row = sendrecv(op, local[lq])
        local[lq] = peer_row
    else:
        # Non-owners do nothing, but keep call sites symmetric
        noop = None


procedure broadcast_row(local, rank, P, owner, k, N):
    m = length(local)
    if rank == owner:
        lr = k - owner * m
        buf = copy_row(local[lr])         # send an owned copy
    else:
        buf = EMPTY_ROW()

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, buf)
        # If I don't have the row yet, take the peer's copy
        if is_empty(buf) and not is_empty(peer):
            buf = peer
        j = j * 2

    return buf


procedure row_owner(global_row, m):
    return floor(global_row / m)

procedure trunc_toward_zero(x):
    if x >= 0: return floor(x)
    else: return -floor(-x)

procedure copy_row(row):
    out = new list(length(row))
    i = 0
    while i < length(row):
        out[i] = row[i]; i = i + 1
    return out

procedure EMPTY_ROW():
    return new list(0)

procedure is_empty(x):
    return length(x) == 0
```

Input size N = 16, processors P = 4.
Starting values: a 16 x 16 upper-triangular integer matrix, using 0-based row and column indices, with entry A[i][j] = 0 when j < i, A[i][j] = 1 when j = i, and A[i][j] = ((17*i + 31*j + 16 + 4) mod 3) - 1 when j > i. It is distributed by contiguous row blocks of 4 rows per processor and stored row-major within each processor.

Definitions:
- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor flattened row-major matrix layout, where each processor stores (N/P) full rows of an N x N matrix. Each subsequent state records one full pivot step k (descending from N-1 to 0): global pivot selection, optional distributed row swap, pivot-row broadcast, and elimination of rows above the pivot (or skip when pivot is zero).
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 3.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

Starting values: the sequence 512 down through 1 distributed contiguously across 8 processors in blocks of size 64.

Definitions:
- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor value layout. S1 is after local ascending sort on each processor. Each subsequent state records one synchronized bitonic compare-split round.
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 7.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Dense N x N matrix, block-row distributed:
#   each rank holds m = N / P consecutive rows
#   rank r owns global rows [r*m .. r*m + m-1]
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure bar(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    N = length(local[0])

    k = N - 1
    while k >= 0:
        (piv_row, piv_val) = global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k)

        if piv_row != k:
            swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, piv_row, k, N)

        barrier()

        piv_owner = row_owner(k, m)
        pivot_row = broadcast_row(local, rank, P, piv_owner, k, N)

        if abs(pivot_row[k]) <= 1e-8:
            barrier()
            k = k - 1
            continue

        r = 0
        while r < m:
            gi = rank * m + r                  # global row index
            if gi < k:                         # only rows above the pivot
                factor = trunc_toward_zero(local[r][k] / pivot_row[k])
                c = 0
                while c <= k:                  # touch only the leading (k+1) columns
                    local[r][c] = local[r][c] - factor * pivot_row[c]
                    c = c + 1
            r = r + 1

        barrier()
        k = k - 1

    return local


procedure global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k):
    m = length(local)
    # Local scan over eligible rows owned here (gi <= k)
    best_abs = -1
    best_idx = -1
    best_val = 0

    r = 0
    while r < m:
        gi = rank * m + r
        if gi <= k:
            v = local[r][k]
            a = abs(v)
            if a > best_abs or (a == best_abs and gi > best_idx):
                best_abs = a
                best_idx = gi
                best_val = v
        r = r + 1

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, (best_abs, best_idx, best_val))

        (p_abs, p_idx, p_val) = peer
        if p_abs > best_abs or (p_abs == best_abs and p_idx > best_idx):
            best_abs = p_abs
            best_idx = p_idx
            best_val = p_val

        j = j * 2

    return (best_idx, best_val)


procedure swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, p, q, N):
    if p == q: return

    m = length(local)
    op = row_owner(p, m)
    oq = row_owner(q, m)
    lp = p - op * m
    lq = q - oq * m

    if op == oq:
        if rank == op:
            tmp = local[lp]; local[lp] = local[lq]; local[lq] = tmp
        return

    if rank == op:
        peer_row = sendrecv(oq, local[lp])
        local[lp] = peer_row
    elif rank == oq:
        peer_row = sendrecv(op, local[lq])
        local[lq] = peer_row
    else:
        # Non-owners do nothing, but keep call sites symmetric
        noop = None


procedure broadcast_row(local, rank, P, owner, k, N):
    m = length(local)
    if rank == owner:
        lr = k - owner * m
        buf = copy_row(local[lr])         # send an owned copy
    else:
        buf = EMPTY_ROW()

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, buf)
        # If I don't have the row yet, take the peer's copy
        if is_empty(buf) and not is_empty(peer):
            buf = peer
        j = j * 2

    return buf


procedure row_owner(global_row, m):
    return floor(global_row / m)

procedure trunc_toward_zero(x):
    if x >= 0: return floor(x)
    else: return -floor(-x)

procedure copy_row(row):
    out = new list(length(row))
    i = 0
    while i < length(row):
        out[i] = row[i]; i = i + 1
    return out

procedure EMPTY_ROW():
    return new list(0)

procedure is_empty(x):
    return length(x) == 0
```

Input size N = 32, processors P = 16.
Starting values: a 32 x 32 upper-triangular integer matrix, using 0-based row and column indices, with entry A[i][j] = 0 when j < i, A[i][j] = 1 when j = i, and A[i][j] = ((17*i + 31*j + 32 + 16) mod 3) - 1 when j > i. It is distributed by contiguous row blocks of 2 rows per processor and stored row-major within each processor.

Definitions:
- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor flattened row-major matrix layout, where each processor stores (N/P) full rows of an N x N matrix. Each subsequent state records one full pivot step k (descending from N-1 to 0): global pivot selection, optional distributed row swap, pivot-row broadcast, and elimination of rows above the pivot (or skip when pivot is zero).
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 15.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

Input size N = 512, processors P = 16.

- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor value layout. S1 is after local ascending sort on each processor. Each subsequent state records one synchronized bitonic compare-split round.
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 15.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P consecutive elements:
#   rank 0 holds a[0 .. m-1], ..., rank P-1 holds a[N-m .. N-1]

procedure compute(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

    block_total = 0
    for x in local: block_total = block_total + x

    i = m - 2
    while i >= 0:
        local[i] = local[i] + local[i + 1]
        i = i - 1

    right = suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P)

    i = 0
    while i < m:
        local[i] = local[i] + right
        i = i + 1

    barrier()
    return local



procedure suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P):
    partial = block_total
    right_sum = 0

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, partial)

        in_lower_half = ((rank & j) == 0)
        if in_lower_half:
            right_sum = right_sum + peer

        partial = partial + peer
        j = j * 2

    return right_sum
```

Input size N = 1024, processors P = 64.
Starting values: the sequence 1 through 1024 distributed contiguously across 64 processors in blocks of size 16.

Definitions:
- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor value layout. Each next state records values after one synchronized update round: either one local reverse-prefix index update, one suffix-carry communication round, or one final right-carry add index update.
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 63.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor value layout. S1 is after local ascending sort on each processor. Each subsequent state records one synchronized bitonic compare-split round.
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 63.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor value layout. S1 is after local ascending sort on each processor. Each subsequent state records one synchronized bitonic compare-split round, followed by a final local reverse on each processor.
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 63.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor value layout. S1 is after local ascending sort on each processor. Each subsequent state records one synchronized bitonic compare-split round.

- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Dense N x N matrix, block-row distributed:
#   each rank holds m = N / P consecutive rows
#   rank r owns global rows [r*m .. r*m + m-1]
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure bar(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    N = length(local[0])

    k = N - 1
    while k >= 0:
        (piv_row, piv_val) = global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k)

        if piv_row != k:
            swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, piv_row, k, N)

        barrier()

        piv_owner = row_owner(k, m)
        pivot_row = broadcast_row(local, rank, P, piv_owner, k, N)

        if abs(pivot_row[k]) <= 1e-8:
            barrier()
            k = k - 1
            continue

        r = 0
        while r < m:
            gi = rank * m + r                  # global row index
            if gi < k:                         # only rows above the pivot
                factor = trunc_toward_zero(local[r][k] / pivot_row[k])
                c = 0
                while c <= k:                  # touch only the leading (k+1) columns
                    local[r][c] = local[r][c] - factor * pivot_row[c]
                    c = c + 1
            r = r + 1

        barrier()
        k = k - 1

    return local


procedure global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k):
    m = length(local)
    # Local scan over eligible rows owned here (gi <= k)
    best_abs = -1
    best_idx = -1
    best_val = 0

    r = 0
    while r < m:
        gi = rank * m + r
        if gi <= k:
            v = local[r][k]
            a = abs(v)
            if a > best_abs or (a == best_abs and gi > best_idx):
                best_abs = a
                best_idx = gi
                best_val = v
        r = r + 1

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, (best_abs, best_idx, best_val))

        (p_abs, p_idx, p_val) = peer
        if p_abs > best_abs or (p_abs == best_abs and p_idx > best_idx):
            best_abs = p_abs
            best_idx = p_idx
            best_val = p_val

        j = j * 2

    return (best_idx, best_val)


procedure swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, p, q, N):
    if p == q: return

    m = length(local)
    op = row_owner(p, m)
    oq = row_owner(q, m)
    lp = p - op * m
    lq = q - oq * m

    if op == oq:
        if rank == op:
            tmp = local[lp]; local[lp] = local[lq]; local[lq] = tmp
        return

    if rank == op:
        peer_row = sendrecv(oq, local[lp])
        local[lp] = peer_row
    elif rank == oq:
        peer_row = sendrecv(op, local[lq])
        local[lq] = peer_row
    else:
        # Non-owners do nothing, but keep call sites symmetric
        noop = None


procedure broadcast_row(local, rank, P, owner, k, N):
    m = length(local)
    if rank == owner:
        lr = k - owner * m
        buf = copy_row(local[lr])         # send an owned copy
    else:
        buf = EMPTY_ROW()

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, buf)
        # If I don't have the row yet, take the peer's copy
        if is_empty(buf) and not is_empty(peer):
            buf = peer
        j = j * 2

    return buf


procedure row_owner(global_row, m):
    return floor(global_row / m)

procedure trunc_toward_zero(x):
    if x >= 0: return floor(x)
    else: return -floor(-x)

procedure copy_row(row):
    out = new list(length(row))
    i = 0
    while i < length(row):
        out[i] = row[i]; i = i + 1
    return out

procedure EMPTY_ROW():
    return new list(0)

procedure is_empty(x):
    return length(x) == 0
```

Definitions:

Each subsequent state records one full pivot step k (descending from N-1 to 0): global pivot selection, optional distributed row swap, pivot-row broadcast, and elimination of rows above the pivot (or skip when pivot is zero).

- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P consecutive elements:
#   rank 0 holds a[0 .. m-1], ..., rank P-1 holds a[N-m .. N-1]

procedure compute(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

    block_total = 0
    for x in local: block_total = block_total + x

    i = m - 2
    while i >= 0:
        local[i] = local[i] + local[i + 1]
        i = i - 1

    right = suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P)

    i = 0
    while i < m:
        local[i] = local[i] + right
        i = i + 1

    barrier()
    return local



procedure suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P):
    partial = block_total
    right_sum = 0

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, partial)

        in_lower_half = ((rank & j) == 0)
        if in_lower_half:
            right_sum = right_sum + peer

        partial = partial + peer
        j = j * 2

    return right_sum
```

Input size N = 1024, processors P = 16.
Starting values: the sequence 1 through 1024 distributed contiguously across 16 processors in blocks of size 64.

Definitions:
- A recorded state means: S0 is the initial per-processor value layout. Each next state records values after one synchronized update round: either one local reverse-prefix index update, one suffix-carry communication round, or one final right-carry add index update.
- T denotes the total number of recorded states for the run.
- S0 is the initial state before any updates.
- States are indexed 0-based: S0 through S_{T-1}.
- Processor indices are 0 through 15.
- Local indices inside each recorded per-processor value list are 0-based.
- L denotes the number of recorded values stored on each processor in a state.
- A1, A2, ... refer to earlier answers in this same chain.
- For any positive modulus M, x mod M is the non-negative remainder in [0, M-1].

Assume each instruction type requires 2 cycles to execute. What is the minimum number of cycles required to execute the program?

Q2: Using the above ideal schedule, what is the value in r5 after cycle [A1]?

Q3: Assume the program is executed on the same ALU, but now the instruction latencies are 2 cycles for add instructions, 2 cycles for sub instructions, 3 cycles for mul instructions, and 1 cycles for mv instructions. Q4: In the ideal program for Q3, what is the most number of instructions issued in a single cycle?

Q5: In the ideal execution schedule found for Q3, what is the utilization? Utilization is the percentage of cycles where a slot is occupied by an issued, but not yet retired instruction.

Q6: Now assume that each ALU slot can be pipelined (i.e. 1 instruction can be issued per cycle on each ALU slot) and new instructions can be issued while executing instructions are in the pipeline. What is the minimum number of cycles to execute the program multiplied by floor([A5*100])?

Give your final answer as: solution = <answer to Q6>

Q6: Now assume that each ALU slot can be pipelined (i.e. 1 instruction can be issued per cycle on each ALU slot) and new instructions can be issued while executing instructions are in the pipeline. What is the minimum number of cycles to execute the program multiplied by floor([A5*100])?

Give your final answer as: solution = <answer to Q6>
